examples.title: Pigeonhole Principle Check Algorithm
examples.description: Algorithm to check if pigeonhole principle guarantees repetition
examples.code:
FUNCTION pigeonhole_check(n, m):
 // n = items, m = containers
 IF n > m THEN
 PRINT "Repetition guaranteed"
 min_in_one_container = CEILING(n / m)
 PRINT "Minimum in one container: " + min_in_one_container
 RETURN TRUE
 ELSE
 PRINT "No guarantee of repetition"
 RETURN FALSE
 END IF

// Example: 10 pigeons, 9 holes
pigeonhole_check(10, 9)
// Output: Repetition guaranteed
// Minimum in one hole: 2
examples.explanation: This algorithm implements the basic pigeonhole principle check. Given n items and m containers, it determines if repetition is guaranteed and calculates the minimum count in the most filled container.

examples.title: Birthday Problem Calculator
examples.description: Calculate minimum people for birthday collision probability
examples.code:
FUNCTION birthday_collision(p):
 // p = desired probability (e.g., 0.5 for 50%)
 n = 1
 probability_no_match = 1.0
 
 WHILE probability_no_match > (1 - p) DO
 probability_no_match = probability_no_match * (365 - n + 1) / 365
 n = n + 1
 END WHILE
 
 PRINT "Minimum people for " + (p*100) + "% chance: " + n
 RETURN n

// Calculate for 50% and 99%
birthday_collision(0.50) // Output: 23
birthday_collision(0.99) // Output: 57
examples.explanation: This algorithm calculates the minimum number of people needed to achieve a given probability of at least two sharing a birthday, demonstrating the birthday paradox phenomenon.

examples.title: Find Minimum Using Generalized Pigeonhole
examples.description: Calculate minimum items in one container using ceiling function
examples.code:
import math

def min_in_one_container(n, m):
 """
 Pigeonhole Principle:
 If n items are placed into m containers,
 at least one container has ceil(n/m) items
 """
 return math.ceil(n / m)

# Examples from common problems
print(min_in_one_container(367, 366)) # Birthday: 2
print(min_in_one_container(27, 26)) # Same initial: 2 
print(min_in_one_container(25, 12)) # Same birth month: 3
print(min_in_one_container(17, 4)) # Same suit cards: 5
print(min_in_one_container(10, 3)) # Basic: 4
examples.explanation: This Python code demonstrates the generalized pigeonhole principle formula. The ceiling function (math.ceil) automatically handles the calculation of minimum items guaranteed in the most filled container for various classic problems.
